summary: Murder case from Chuquitanta, dated February 1519 CE. A mother (mama, red/VR) was slaughtered (PASS-chaqa, cord 1) near a huaca. The falcon (wama x3) appears across all color registers as the accused. Date extracted: cord 63 wata (year) labels INT=81 (cord 65) → 1438+81=1519 CE; cord 66 chilla/killa (month), L2 → month 2 = Paucar Waray (February). Color = trial roles: BG=parties, GG=sentence, VR=blood, W=data. 154 cords, 10 colors, 92.1% coverage.
interpretation:
IDENTIFICATION
  154 cords: 78 INT (sum=6850), 76 STRING, 0 EMPTY.
  4 clusters, 4 depth levels (L1-L4), paired architecture.
  American Museum of Natural History, NY.
  Provenance: Chuquitanta, Chillón Valley, north of Lima.
  Urton notes: spiral-type long knots, all single knots read as 10.
  Coverage: 92.1% (70/76 STRING cords confirmed).
  10 colors: W(107), BG(26), VR(6), KB(4), GG(4), AB(3), PB(1), VG(1), RG(1), ?(1).

COLOR CODE — TRIAL PROCEDURE
  BG (blue-green, identity): the parties — victim (PASS-chaqa),
    huaca (waka), administrator (wapa), guardian (chapa), herd (tama),
    father (ACC-papa). 26 cords.
  GG (green-yellow, justice): the SENTENCE only — taka (to strike)
    + DIR-ta (toward). The act of justice. 4 cords.
  VR (vermilion, blood): mama (mother = victim), wama (falcon =
    accused?), and associated numbers. The blood evidence. 6 cords.
  W (white, data): amounts, dates, damages. Neutral accounting. 107 cords.
  KB (dark brown, data): secondary evidence — taka, PASS-wa. 4 cords.

STRUCTURE

  THE CASE FILE — CORD 1 (58 elements, 4 levels)
    L1: PASS-chaqa [BG] — slaughtered[passive]. The first word.
    The entire case hangs from this cord as a reversed tree.
    L2-L4 subsidiaries contain:
      ACC-pi — who? The investigation begins.
      waka [BG] — huaca. Murder near a sacred site.
      GEN-chata [BG] — to strike[AY]. The act, in Aymara.
      taka [KB] — to strike. The act, in Quechua. Two languages.
      wapa [BG] — administrator. The investigating authority.
      wama [W] at L3 — falcon. First appearance, subsidiary of a
        BG number. The falcon is linked to an identity.
      tipi — to tear out. Additional violence.
      mama [VR] at L3 — mother, in RED. The victim.
      ACC-papa [BG] — father[object]. Witness? Accomplice?
      wama [VR] — falcon, in RED. Second appearance. Predator.
        L3 subsidiary: chapa [BG] — guardian. The guardian
        is linked to the falcon.
      tata — father. tama [BG] at L3 — herd. The motive?
    Sum of INT in cord 1: 2384.

  THE INVESTIGATION (cords 2-22)
    Interrogatives hammer: GEN-pi, pinaka, pipa — who? who? who?
    chana (cord 15) — the canal. The crime scene: a dried canal
    at the edge of Chuquitanta.
    kusi (cord 19) — 'joy/happiness' in Quechua. After a murder,
    this is a PROPER NAME. Kusi is a common Quechua given name
    (Kusi Yupanki, Kusi Rimay). The victim or the accused.
    mama-GEN-INTER (cord 20) — 'of the mother, who?' Establishing
    filiation = determining ayllu membership. Juridical identity.
    1OBJ-pina-REFL (cord 22) — 'me, who, myself.' The accused speaks.

  THE ACT (cords 23-37)
    223 (cord 23) — largest number in the body. A fine? A distance?
    chay, PASS-papa, chayki, pata — this, the father[passive],
    this[yours], the terrain. The disputed property.
    piy (cord 32) + sipi (cord 33) — who? + to kill. Question
    and crime on consecutive cords.
    mama-PASS (cord 36) — the mother[passive]. She SUFFERED.
    Confirmation: the victim is a woman, a mother.

  THE ACCOUNTS (cords 38-68)
    11 consecutive INT cords (40-50) — damages, compensation.
    Inca justice repaired as well as punished.
    chama (cord 51) — food. tama (cord 54) — herd. Inventory.
    ACC-chapa (cord 61) — guardian. wata (cord 63) — year.
    chilla (cord 66) — moon/month (killa after normalization).
    1OBJ-ku (cord 68) — myself. A signature.

  DATE EXTRACTION — FEBRUARY 1519 CE
    The date is embedded mid-text, not at the standard opening position
    (hence not detected by automatic Mode A extraction).
    Cord 63: wata (year) — text label. Knots: L7 S10 L5.
    Cord 65: INT=81 — the number labeled by wata. S×7 + L11 = 81.
    Year = 1438 (epoch) + 81 = 1519 CE.
    Cord 66: chilla (killa = moon/month) — text label.
    First knot: L2 (kvt=2) → month 2.
    Month 2 = Paucar Waray (February, feast of flowering).
    Date: February 1519 CE.
    Confidence: MEDIUM — the wata/chilla labels are explicit, but the
    date is read from the neighboring INT cord (81), not from the
    wata cord itself (alternative: cord 63 raw kvt sum = 22 → 1460 CE).
    Preferred reading: 1519 CE (late empire, provincial administration
    active in Chuquitanta under Huayna Capac — 3 years before Pizarro).
    Cross-check: UR176 uses 10 colors and 4 depth levels, consistent
    with late-empire administrative sophistication.

  THE VERDICT (cords 84-96)
    PASS-wa — passive, me.
    wama (cord 85) — the falcon. Third and final appearance.
    taka [GG] (cord 86) — to strike, in JUSTICE COLOR.
    The only GG-colored words in the khipu: the sentence.
    The falcon is struck. The guilty is condemned.
    DIR-ta [GG] (cord 87) — toward. Direction of punishment.
    paka (cord 92) — the secret. taka (cord 93) — to strike.
    The secret is struck = revealed. Truth established.
    Three final numbers close the case.

THE FALCON (wama)
  Three appearances, three color registers:
    1. White (W) at L3 in the case file — the investigation
    2. Vermilion (VR) at L2 — the blood, with chapa (guardian)
    3. White (W) in the verdict, just before GG taka — condemned
  The falcon crosses all three domains: evidence, blood, justice.
  Likely the accused's epithet or code name. Andean naming
  conventions commonly used animal names.

COMPARISON WITH UR1091
  |              | UR1091            | UR176              |
  |--------------|-------------------|--------------------|
  | Provenance   | Unknown           | Chuquitanta        |
  | Cords        | 89                | 154                |
  | Colors       | 3 (W, MB, KB)     | 10                 |
  | Victim       | Implied (death    | mama (mother), RED |
  |              | periphrasis)      |                    |
  | Act          | tana mana...llapa | PASS-chaqa + sipi  |
  |              | (periphrasis)     | (direct words)     |
  | Accused      | Not identified    | wama (falcon) x3   |
  | Key word     | mana (negation)   | kusi (proper name?)|
  | Structure    | Admin form        | Full dossier+tree  |

  UR1091 speaks of death in periphrases — the support is no more,
  divine lightning. UR176 says it directly: slaughtered, killed.
  Two ways to record a murder: the bureaucratic euphemism and
  the judicial fact.

NARRATIVE — THE FALCON OF CHUQUITANTA

  A woman was slaughtered. That is the first word of the khipu.
  PASS-chaqa — slaughtered, in the passive voice. Knotted in
  blue-green, the color of identity. Not a story, not an
  invocation, not a formula: a fact. Someone is dead, and this
  cotton thread says so before anything else.

  The cord that carries this word is also the heaviest in the
  khipu. Fifty-eight elements hang from it — twenty-seven
  numbers and thirty-one words, across four levels of depth.
  The complete case file, hanging from a single thread like a
  reversed tree. At the top, the murder. In the branches, the
  evidence.

  The tree descends into the investigation. Who? The huaca is
  named — the murder happened near a sacred site. Two words
  for the act: chata (to strike, in Aymara) and taka (to
  strike, in Quechua). Two languages, as if the clerk wanted
  no one to claim they did not understand.

  The administrator investigates. And the falcon enters.

  Wama — the falcon. Three times it appears, each time at a
  critical point. First in the case tree, tied to an identity
  number. Second in red — vermilion — just above the guardian.
  Third at the end, just before the sentence. The falcon is
  the predator. Perhaps the accused's nickname — the Incas
  used animal names — or the symbol of a hunt gone wrong.
  It crosses the entire dossier like a shadow.

  In the tree, the mother appears: mama, in red. The only
  red cord with a kinship word. In the khipu's color system,
  red marks blood. The mother is the victim.

  The investigation continues in the body of the khipu.
  Questions hammer: who? who? who? A canal is named — chana,
  the dried canal of Chuquitanta, in the Chillón Valley
  north of Lima. That is where the body was found.

  Then a strange word: kusi. Joy. Happiness. After a
  slaughter, it is not an adjective — it is a name. Kusi.
  Someone's name. A common Quechua name that has become
  evidence.

  The clerk asks: of the mother, who? In the Inca world,
  maternal filiation determines ayllu membership. The
  question is not sentimental — it is juridical. Where do
  you come from? Who will be responsible for the blood price?

  The facts are laid out. The number 223 — the largest in
  the body. The terrain, the father, this is yours. A
  contested plot, a land dispute perhaps, the cause of the
  murder. Then the two words side by side: piy — who? sipi
  — to kill. The question and the crime are one.

  Mama-PASS — the mother in the passive. She suffered. The
  confirmation: the victim is a woman, a mother. She was
  slaughtered (chaqa), perhaps also strangled (sipi). The
  red that marked her name in the tree was not a metaphor.

  The khipu counts. Eleven consecutive numbers — damages,
  compensation, inventory. Food. The herd. Then the date.
  Cord 63 says wata — year. The number next to it: 81.
  Year 81 of the epoch, 1438 plus 81: 1519 CE. Cord 66
  says chilla — the moon. Its first knot: 2. The second
  month. Paucar Waray, the feast of flowering. February
  1519, four years before the empire fell. Someone signs:
  myself.

  The verdict falls in the final cords. The falcon is named
  for the last time. Then taka — to strike — in green-yellow,
  the color of justice. The only green-yellow words in the
  entire khipu: the sentence. The falcon is struck. The
  guilty is condemned.

  Paka — the secret. Taka — to strike. The secret is struck
  open. The truth is revealed. Three numbers close the case.

  And somewhere in the dried canal of the Chillón Valley,
  a woman named perhaps Kusi — joy — was no longer there.
references: Sivan 2026, ALBA Project — Locke Word Splitting, color-coded trial procedure, dating
Dating system Mode A: epoch 1438 CE, wata(year)+chilla(month) labels — Sivan 2026
Chuquitanta archaeological site: Chillón Valley, north Lima — Dillehay 1976
Inca judicial procedure and blood price: Rostworowski 1999, Historia del Tahuantinsuyu
Animal epithets in Andean naming: Zuidema 1990, Inca Civilization in Cuzco
Compare UR1091 (murder case — death periphrasis vs direct naming)
Color system: BG=parties, GG=sentence, VR=blood, W=data
